text: Robotic ports are placed, and the surgeon assesses the peritoneum, revealing macronodular liver cirrhosis.
The surgeon uses suction to remove gastrointestinal spillage and fibrinous exudate from the abdominal cavity, clearing the area for further repair of the duodenal ulcer perforation.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection and inspects the hepato-duodenal area to visually identify the site of the duodenal ulcer perforation.
The surgeon identifies a perforation on the anterior wall of the duodenum, located near the duodenal bulb.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 silk suture to repair the duodenal ulcer perforation, carefully stitching the tissue to close the defect.
The surgeon places full-thickness sutures approximately 0.5 centimeters away from the friable edges of the duodenal perforation, securing the tissue from one margin to the other.
The surgeon takes a suture bite that includes the posterior duodenal wall, ensuring the repair encompasses both anterior and posterior aspects of the duodenal perforation.
The surgeon mobilizes a patch of viable omentum and positions it without tension over the perforation on the anterior wall of the duodenum.
The surgeon ties the sutures to securely anchor the mobilized omental patch over the perforation on the anterior wall of the duodenum.
The surgeon adjusts the tension on the sutures to stabilize the omental patch over the perforation, ensuring it is secure while maintaining adequate blood supply to the omentum.
The surgical field shows no drainage performed in the subhepatic space, with the omental patch visibly covering the perforation site.
The surgeon irrigates the peritoneal cavity with a large volume of warm saline, ensuring thorough cleansing of the surgical area.
The surgeon irrigates the suprahepatic and subhepatic recesses and pelvis with saline to remove any remaining contamination.
